content.text: Cross-Team Dependency Registry for Claude Initiatives

At enterprise scale, Claude deployments rarely live inside a single team. The Finance AI initiative needs the Platform team's API gateway. The Sales AI proposal generator depends on a RAG pipeline owned by Data Engineering. When these dependencies are tracked only in emails and Jira comments, a blocked handoff doesn't surface until a demo is days away. A cross-team dependency registry makes every inter-team commitment visible in one place and keeps the program manager informed in real time.

attrs.alt: Cross-team dependency map showing initiative dependencies through the Platform team

content.text: What Belongs in a Dependency Registry
A dependency is any commitment where one team must deliver a capability before another team's work can proceed. For Claude programs these commonly include:
— Platform provisioning Claude API keys, rate limits, or model tier upgrades
— Data Engineering building or certifying a RAG index that a product team will query
— Security or Legal reviewing a new tool integration before it reaches production
— A prompt library, evaluation harness, or guardrail layer built once and consumed by multiple initiatives
— Vector DB provisioning, GPU quota, or VPC peering that enables agent memory or fine-tuning
Track the dependency at the point where a missed deadline in Team A blocks Team B — not the internal tasks within each team.
Each dependency entry captures the five things a program manager needs to assess health at a glance: who owns it, who needs it, what the capability is, when it's due, and whether it's on track.

Status fields alone don't capture nuance. A "blocked" dependency three weeks before its consumer needs it is very different from one that blocks a demo tomorrow. Claude assesses the risk level and recommends escalation path by reasoning over the status note and surrounding program context:

A weekly dependency scan should take less than five minutes to produce and fit in a single Slack message. Claude reads the full registry and writes a structured health narrative that highlights what program leadership needs to act on:

def generate_weekly_dep_report(registry: DependencyRegistry) -> str:
    """Generate a plain-English weekly dependency report using Claude."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    blocked_txt = registry.critical_summary()
    dep_list = "\n".join(
        f"- [{d.dep_id}] {d.provider_team}→{d.consumer_team}: {d.capability} | "
        f"due {d.due_date} | {d.status.value}"
        for d in registry.deps
    )

    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
You are a program manager writing a weekly cross-team dependency status report.

Program: {registry.subject} — Stage: {registry.stage}

All dependencies:
{dep_list}

Critical items:
{blocked_txt}

Write a concise weekly status report (≤200 words) covering:
1. Overall health (RAG/AMBER/RED) with one-line rationale
2. Blocked items and who must act
3. At-risk items to watch
4. Recommended actions for this week

Use plain English. No bullet overload — group logically.
"""}],
    )
    return message.content[0].text

# Example
reg = DependencyRegistry(subject="Claude Enterprise", stage="Q3-2025")
reg.add(Dependency("DEP-018", "Platform", "Finance AI", "Claude API",
                    date(2025, 8, 30), DepStatus.ON_TRACK))
reg.add(Dependency("DEP-027", "Platform", "HR AI", "Model Access",
                    date(2025, 9, 10), DepStatus.AT_RISK,
                    notes="GPU quota request pending approval for 2 weeks."))
reg.add(Dependency("DEP-044", "Platform", "Sales AI", "RAG Pipeline",
                    date(2025, 9, 15), DepStatus.BLOCKED,
                    notes="Security review not started."))
report = generate_weekly_dep_report(reg)
Drafting Resolution Plans for Blocked Items
For each blocked dependency, Claude drafts a 3-step resolution plan so the program manager arrives at the escalation call with options, not just a problem description:

def draft_resolution_plan(dep: Dependency) -> str:
    """Draft an unblock plan for a blocked dependency."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    message = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"""
Dependency [{dep.dep_id}] is BLOCKED.
Provider: {dep.provider_team} | Consumer: {dep.consumer_team}
Capability: {dep.capability} | Due: {dep.due_date}
Notes: {dep.notes}

Write a 3-step resolution plan:
1. Immediate action (this week)
2. Short-term fix (1–2 weeks)
3. Long-term prevention

Keep each step ≤ 25 words. Be specific and actionable.
"""}],
    )
    return message.content[0].text
Unique identifier, used in all communications
Team that must deliver the capability
Date by which provider must deliver
Director within 24h; Exec if unresolved in 3 days
Registry Lifecycle: From Intake to Closure
Dependencies are registered during initiative intake, not at project kickoff. This gives the program office visibility into the commitment load each providing team carries before work begins.
: When a team submits a new Claude initiative, they list the external capabilities they need and the due dates they require them by. Program management converts these into registry entries.
: The providing team acknowledges the commitment and sets a status. Any "at risk" flag at this stage triggers an immediate conversation.
: A script queries the registry and uses Claude to generate the weekly health report. Blocked items auto-escalate.
Dependency review in the monthly PI sync
: The full registry is reviewed in the Program Increment planning session. Clusters of at-risk dependencies from the same provider indicate a capacity problem, not just individual slippage.
: When a consumer team confirms the capability works in their environment, the dependency moves to resolved. Both teams sign off.
Teams discover a blocker only at integration
Require dependency listing during intake form
Provider claims they were never asked
Registry shows on_track while delivery is late
Weekly automated ping to provider lead for update
Track only cross-team dependencies, not internal tasks
Consumer team says 'yes' but can't use it yet
Require a working integration test before closure
Integration with the Broader Program Cadence
The dependency registry is not a standalone artifact. It feeds into three broader program rituals:
: Blocked dependencies are highlighted as risks against KRs that depend on the blocked capability.
: High dependency load on a providing team (e.g. Platform owns 14 active dependencies) surfaces as a capacity constraint during quarterly planning.
: If a dependency slips its due date, the multi-quarter roadmap is adjusted to reflect the downstream delay before the consumer team loses planning confidence.

When the registry grows beyond twenty entries, scanning it row-by-row is slow. A heat map aggregates dependency counts by provider team so leadership sees at a glance which teams are overcommitted as providers — a leading indicator of future blockages.

from collections import Counter

def dependency_heat_map(registry: DependencyRegistry) -> dict:
    """Return provider load counts split by status."""
    load: dict[str, Counter] = {}
    for dep in registry.deps:
        team = dep.provider_team
        if team not in load:
            load[team] = Counter({"on_track": 0, "at_risk": 0, "blocked": 0})
        load[team][dep.status.value] += 1
    return load

def print_heat_map(registry: DependencyRegistry) -> None:
    hm = dependency_heat_map(registry)
    print(f"{'Provider':<25} {'On-Track':>10} {'At-Risk':>10} {'Blocked':>10} {'Total':>8}")
    print("-" * 65)
    for team, counts in sorted(hm.items(), key=lambda x: -sum(x[1].values())):
        total = sum(counts.values())
        risk_flag = " ⚠" if counts["at_risk"] + counts["blocked"] > 2 else ""
        print(
            f"{team:<25} {counts['on_track']:>10} {counts['at_risk']:>10}"
            f" {counts['blocked']:>10} {total:>8}{risk_flag}"
        )

# Example output:
# Provider                  On-Track    At-Risk    Blocked    Total
# -----------------------------------------------------------------
# Platform Infra                   4          2          1        7 ⚠
# Data Engineering                 2          1          0        3
# Security                         3          0          0        3

A provider team showing more than two at-risk or blocked dependencies simultaneously should trigger a capacity conversation in the next PI planning session — they are likely being asked to do more than their bandwidth allows.
Program Increment (PI) planning is the highest-leverage moment to review and renegotiate dependencies. The dependency registry feeds the PI planning session in three ways:
: The heat map identifies which teams are over-subscribed as providers before the sprint plan is locked.

: Each dependency due date is checked against the consumer team's sprint plan. If the consumer team needs the capability in Sprint 3 but the provider's due date is Sprint 4, the gap is renegotiated on the spot.
: All at-risk and blocked items are placed on the PI planning risk board. The program manager owns driving them to resolution during the PI.
Each team reviews their consumed dependencies
Vote deferred if critical deps unresolved

Manually updating dependency status is the single biggest reason registries become stale. The most reliable automation pattern is a webhook that fires when the provider team closes a Jira ticket or merges a pull request tagged with the dependency ID:

from flask import Flask, request, jsonify
import json

app = Flask(__name__)
registry: DependencyRegistry = load_registry_from_db()  # your persistence layer

@app.route("/webhook/dep-update", methods=["POST"])
def dep_update():
    payload = request.json
    dep_id = payload.get("dep_id")          # e.g. "DEP-044"
    new_status = payload.get("status")      # e.g. "resolved"
    note = payload.get("note", "")

    dep = next((d for d in registry.deps if d.dep_id == dep_id), None)
    if not dep:
        return jsonify({"error": "dep_id not found"}), 404

    old_status = dep.status
    dep.status = DepStatus(new_status)
    dep.notes = note
    save_registry_to_db(registry)

    # If newly blocked, immediately trigger escalation classification
    if dep.status == DepStatus.BLOCKED and old_status != DepStatus.BLOCKED:
        ctx = f"Webhook update received. Previous status: {old_status.value}."
        result = classify_dependency_risk(dep, context=ctx)
        if result["escalate"]:
            notify_escalation(dep, result)  # send Slack / email

    return jsonify({"ok": True, "dep_id": dep_id, "status": new_status}), 200

def notify_escalation(dep: Dependency, assessment: dict) -> None:
    """Send Slack notification to the escalation owner."""
    message = (
        f"⛔ *Dependency Blocked* [{dep.dep_id}]\n"
        f"Provider: {dep.provider_team} → Consumer: {dep.consumer_team}\n"
        f"Capability: {dep.capability} | Due: {dep.due_date}\n"
        f"Risk: {assessment['risk_summary']}\n"
        f"Action: {assessment['action']}\n"
        f"Escalation Level: {assessment['level']}"
    )
    # post_to_slack(channel="#program-escalations", text=message)
    print(message)

Each time a provider team moves a ticket to Done in Jira, the webhook fires, the registry updates automatically, and if the status change indicates a new block, Claude classifies the risk and fires an escalation notification before the program manager's morning standup.

A cross-team dependency registry replaces ad-hoc status chasing with a structured, automated system. The data model captures who owes what to whom and by when. Claude handles the analytical work: risk classification, weekly health narrative generation, and resolution plan drafting. The result is a program manager who arrives at every review meeting with a complete picture of cross-team commitments — and the time to address problems rather than discover them.
